sentences.text: My name is Aaron Elster, A-A-R-O-N.
Last name is E-L-S-T-E-R.
My Jewish name was Nachman Aaron, which is I dropped the Nachman, and I'm using the Aaron, which is-- which is what I'm using.
Yes.
I was born on February 4, 1933.
However, I'm not sure whether that's exactly the date I was born.
That's the date I've been using ever since I came to the United States.
I could be half a year older, a year older perhaps, not younger.
Based on February 4, 1933, I'm 62.
I could be 62 or 63.
I was born in a town called Sokolow Podlaski.
Sokolow is spelled S-O-K-O-L-O-W.
The O has a little slash on it, and the L has a slash on it.
It's  a soft L, Sokolow.
Podlaski is P-O-D-L-A-S-K-I, Sokolow Podlaski.
Poland.
Next-sized town was a town called Siedlce, Vengrov.
Those were the towns around us, probably by-- probably 80, 90 miles from Warsaw.
The town, to the best of my recollection, had a population of approximately 12,000 people.
By the beginning of the war, by 1939, there were probably between 4-- 5,000 Jews in that town.
The town, when the ghetto started, there were approximately 6,000 Jews in the town because they chased older Jews out of the farm areas in the vicinities, and they brought them all into the town.
So therefore, there probably were about 6,000 Jews in    there.
And what kind of town was this?
Was this an agricultural town, an urban kind of place?
The town was surrounded with farms.
But the town itself with the Jewish population, there were a lot of trades-- tailors, shoemakers, store owners.
I remember there were a couple of schools.
There were two schools.
There was also three synagogues that I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
There was a yeshiva there that I recall.
Yeshiva is a-- I would imagine it would be sort of rabbinical-- preparation for the-- for the rabbinical.
The town, from my recollection, was a-- the Jews were fairly religious there, or most of them were fairly religious.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The normal things as a child that I remember is just my surrounding areas.
Later on, by '39 or '40, I remember there was a movie house even there.
We still didn't have inside plumbing.
[LAUGHS] But that was part of our life and never knew that anything else existed.
So we didn't feel like we were deprived of anything.
I would say my childhood was fairly normal, being based on those surroundings and circumstances.
Ah, what does a child do at five, six, seven years of age?
I went to cheder, which is Hebrew school.
And how do you spell that?
I never had a chance to-- Hebrew school?
How do I--
Cheder.
Well, cheder is Hebrew school.
It's-- you learn how to-- how to pray in Hebrew, how to read Hebrew, and we had a-- a teacher.
But I do remember the teacher we used to call-- and I think it was called a melamed.
And the traditional was you sat around the table and you repeated what he said, and you read from a book.
And if you didn't do well, he would take his ruler and give you one over the head.
[LAUGHS] But that was common.
It happened to me.
I got thrown out of cheder one time.
In fact, he was my uncle, with a big, long, red beard.
He lived upstairs of us, and he-- the cheder was right in his apartment.
I recollect that.
I never went to public school, because by the time I was old enough to go to public school, the war was on, and I-- we, as Jews, we couldn't go to public school.
So my education just prior to the war was limited to religious education.
My formal education really didn't start till after the war.
My family had a butcher shop.
My dad and my mother both worked in the butcher shop.
In fact, it was sort of unusual, but my family's shop was-- the clientele was mostly Gentile because our meats were not kosher.
That means it was not for Jewish consumption.
So most of our customers were either the farmers or the Poles in-- in-- within the city.
And therefore, our acquaintances were-- a lot of were Polish people.
And that's probably one of the reasons for my and my sister's survival, because of our acquaintances with the Gentile population.
Apart from your butcher shop business, were you associated with Gentiles at your age, as a child?
Personally, I don't even know whether they were Gentile or Jews.
There were kids that we played with.
But I do recall the revelation of a Gentile boy, you know, coming up to me and asking me why I killed Christ.
And at that time, I didn't know who Christ was.
So I went home and asked my parents who Christ is, and why is he saying that to me?
That was probably my first encounter with that type of situation.
But I lived basically in a Jewish community.
So we were pretty well confined to our area.
We did mingle with the outside community.
Were you aware of anti-Semitism?
Not at that age, not till basically when the war started.
About 1939, that's when I became more aware of it.
I was more aware of other feelings, I was-- of religious conflicts that I had as a child.
And that started very young with me, and I'm-- I'm very surprised at that.
I was supposedly very skinny.
And my mother used to want to fatten me up, so she would slip-- take me in the back yard and slip some non-kosher meats.
But then I would have nightmares that I would be going to hell, that I would be-- because I ate treyf.
And in cheder you learn that if you committed any type of sins that there was a ladder to go up to heaven.
And any time you committed a sin, one of the rungs would be gone, and you'd never make it up to heaven.
And I think I must have been five, six years old, and I was already preoccupied with death and-- and heaven and hell.
My God was a very vengeful, vengeful God.
That's my recollection.
My dad was.
My mother wasn't.
She came from a family of-- of 12 children, and more boisterous type and rowdy.
My dad was sort of on the quiet side, sedate.
He came, I think, from a more religious family.
In fact, his father-- I was named after his dad, and my sister was named after his mother.
My grandfather's name was Nachman Aaron, and naturally I never knew him.
So there was somewhat of a conflict, I think, in their ideology.
And from what I understand as a kid, I think it was also a fixed marriage because she was more the aggressive type and the outgoing, and he was the opposite.
So I think it was-- the marriage was sort of fixed.
But-- So how did the butcher shop come about to be a non-kosher butcher shop?
That I don't know, that I don't know.
I would imagine it was a business decision.
It was less competition in the area.
And perhaps they felt that this was the best way to make a living.
I-- I don't-- you know, there was a lot of kosher butcher shops.
In fact, my grandfather was a butcher, and some of my uncles had butcher shops.
But my-- my mother's sister, aunt-- I had an aunt-- I have an-- I had an aunt, she also had a butcher shop.
So I don't know how that came about, probably for business reasons.
But I do remember going into the butcher shop all the time, in fact, carrying meats out, delivering, seeing people come on the [POLISH] on their horse-drawn wagons, park in front of the store.

And then I also remember the-- that started happening in 1939 or so, where Poles would come and paste stickers on the customers' back with a sign saying, swinia-- swinia in Polish meaning pig-- for buying from Jews.
OK?
And then on the other hand, the religious people didn't like us having treyf meat.
So there was always a conflict.
I don't know how it got started, but I just happen to remember that now that you mention that.
There was a conflict with that.
But our home life, I think, was fairly normal.
I didn't have any wants or needs for anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had uncles that used to make toys for me.
I'd gladly give them my food, and they would trade me for-- for toys.
In fact, I remember one thing.
One uncle of mine used to make his out of roof shingles.
He used to make like a rifle with a rubber band.
And you would put a piece of wood on there, and you could shoot  with it.
I thought it was quite-- quite nice.
That was in probably '39 when-- just about when the war started.
In fact, I remember when the war started, the bombs.
The Germans attacked, and the bombs came down.
I was in cheder, and we all ran out  of cheder, went into the woods.
That was my first experience with that.
And my parents also ran out.
And we were laying in the wooded area, and the bombs were falling.
And the airplanes, they seemed so tremendous.
The noise was so powerful.
And it was the first encounter I've ever seen an airplane.
I never saw an airplane.
In fact, I saw very few cars because mostly everything was horse and-- horse and buggy.
So my realization of things began after the-- after the war started.
That's where my memory is.
OK.
I began to realize what was happening, I would say, closer to 1940.
There were a lot of Jews that were thrown out of Germany.
So some Jews came to different parts of Poland, and I remember there was one German Jew that lived in the back of our house.
And at night, the neighbors used to get together.
And they used to come into the house, and they used to talk about all the terrible things that was going to befall the Jews and that was already happening to Jews.

And as a child, I used to sit in the corner and listen to all this conversation and absorb it all and be very, very frightened, because they were predicting terrible things that were going to happen to-- to-- to the Jews, and mostly from people from the outside.
Well, number one, they were chasing the Jews out of Germany.
They were putting them in concentration camps.
They were building ghettos, those type of things.
And then later on, the rumors started coming around about killing of Jews.
And people didn't believe it.
Somewhat and somewhat not.
Because nobody would believe that this type of Holocaust was going to happen, that that type of wholesale slaughter could possibly happen.
Jews in Europe were used to pogroms, and anti-Semitism, and-- and from time to time, a pogrom to happen, that somebody was killed, or people were hurt, or their stores were damaged.
But not that-- not to the extent that that was going on.
In my childhood--
But they didn't really touch me.
I was not-- I wasn't touched by that.
It was mostly stories that I heard from my parents and from family and from-- from neighbors.
I have one sister that's slightly older than I am.
And I had a younger sister that was about two years younger than me.
She died in Treblinka.
And there was only-- there was three of us, and naturally my parents.
My father's name was Chaim Sruel, and my mother's name was Cywia.
And as I indicated, she came from a family of 12 children.
There's two of them that still survive.
One lives in New York.
His name is Sam Scherb.
And there's one in Israel by the name of Sruel Scherb.
There was another one by the name of Malka Goldstein.
She is now deceased.
They escaped to Russia in 1939 when the Russians were retreating.
And the Germans and the-- and the Russians were at the-- they were there in the same time in our town.
When they were retreating, a lot the-- a lot of the Jews, quite a few Jews escaped to Russia because they anticipated what was going to happen.
But a lot of people didn't believe it, especially people who had businesses and had homes, and they just didn't feel that they should be leaving that.
And my parents apparently decided to stay.
Ah, 1939, our business still continued.
Slightly, slowly, things started happening.
Jews had to start wearing armbands, and there were rations to start with.
And then the people were gathered from the outskirts of the city and brought into the town.
A certain part of the town was allocated towards a ghetto.
And then about 1940, they started putting walls around the ghetto at the main entrances and on the streets.
And the fields, there was barbed wire with guards walking around it.
Oh, yes, oh, yes.
I was-- I was extremely mature, I don't know why, at a very young age.
And I'll-- I'll touch on it in a-- in a second.
I just want to tell you about the-- the commerce that was going on while the ghetto-- while in the ghetto.
And I remember they used to smuggle beef in through the barbed wire by cutting the fences.
And I remember, as a kid, there were two, three people.
They would slaughter a cow in a barn.
And all of a sudden, there was meat for people.
We had-- we had food to eat-- to eat during those times.
I never felt any-- any-- I didn't feel any problems.
But I do know people were walking about begging.
There was people sitting around not having anything to eat.
But somehow my parents were able to manage during those times to provide food and shelter for us.
I was frightened of what was going on because of what I was hearing.
I was frightened that-- I believed that they were going to kill the Jews.
And the stories were coming around that there were concentration camps.
There were crematoriums, that they were burning Jews.
They were killing Jews, putting them into mass graves and so on.
And that frightened me very much.
In fact, I used to-- I used to say to God, you know, why?
I couldn't have been more than maybe seven, eight years of age.
Why?
And I always wanted to be spared.
And I would try to make deals in my own way with God to spare me.
But yet, after I would do all that, I would say to myself, why would God want to spare you?
After all, there are more children that are much more pious than you.
So why would he spare you?
So there was sort of a conflict, wanting to live and yet having feelings that, why would God make an exception and let me survive?
And that's never escaped me.
It's been with me ever since I was a small kid.
What kind of deals would you try to make with  God?
Well, that I was going to be a good person.
I was going to do good deeds.
And the normal type of promises that you promise when you want something.
But yet, for some odd reason, I would always fight that in my own self because I didn't feel I was worthy of it.
I would say, why would He do that?
I thought I always did, except for the times when somebody used to feed me some treyf food, which I wasn't supposed to eat.
After all, what kind of troubles could I possibly get into at seven years of age or eight years of age?
Went to cheder, did whatever I was supposed to do.
But we started seeing things happening within the ghetto, and I remember that.
See, Treblinka, the death camp Treblinka, was only about 17 to 20 miles away from my town.
And they formed a committee.
There was a committee within the ghetto.
There was a president.
There was police.
There was a whole government.
And the Germans would demand a certain number of people to go to Treblinka to help build the camps or help expand on the camps, because Treblinka existed as a labor camp before.
And nobody ever came back.
And I also remember my dad being on the list to go to Treblinka for labor purposes.
But like anything else, he was able to-- you could always-- somehow he made a deal to-- to-- to get out of that-- get off that list.
That was probably 1941.
Yes, yes.
It became a very, very fearful-- rumors, rumors.
Nobody came back from Treblinka.
But it was all rumors.
And--
Oh, yeah, oh, yeah.
But nobody believed the extent of it.
Nobody believed the extent of it.
People walking to their deaths wouldn't believe that it was going to happen.
Something was going to interfere.
Somebody was going to save them.
And I remember, in the ghetto now, my sister used to be the courier.
She used to deliver the meats on the outside, sneak through the gates.
My older sister, yeah.
And it was easier for her to do it because as a child, as a young girl, if you didn't look Jewish nobody bothered you.
But as a boy, it was much more difficult because of the circumcision.
All they have to do is take your pants down, they knew you were Jewish.
Because in Poland nobody was circumcised in those years.
So she was more involved with the business than-- than I, helping out.
But I also remember the black market.
And the gendarmes used to come in and-- in the ghetto.
And there was one guy by the name of Lolek, never forget him.
He was a Polish national that lived on the German border.
They were called, I believe, Volksdeutchen, and he was in the gendarmes.
And one day, he came to the house, dragged my dad out of bed about 2:00, 3:00 in the morning.
And in  a hallway-- they used to have like a two-by-four to guard doors.
And he took that two-by-four and proceeded to beat my dad with it, broke his arm.
Why?
Because he didn't pay off.
Somebody squealed on him.
That I do remember.
I remember my dad walking around with the sling on him.
And I felt-- I-- I felt-- I felt terrible for my dad.
I've always felt extreme compassion for my father.
He represented something very soft and very idealistic and so on.
Not so my mother.
My mother was more the pusher and shover and so on.
So it was a different relationship, always.
All these years, I still picture him the same way.
I remember one time he had to have a tooth pulled, and there was no such thing as Novocaine or whatever.
So he didn't go to the shop that day.
And I remember he came home and took a bottle of liquor and started drinking it.
And I would help him.
And then he made, silly thing, he made a bunch of fried eggs.
We both ate them, and that's a day I had with him which I-- I cherished for a long time.
[SOBS]
That was probably in '41.
So I mean, you know, there were bad things happening.
But my family, for some odd reason, was able to stay together.
And what happened, then, in 1942, when the ghetto was totally closed, you noticed the-- the harsher things that were happening.
People being pulled out, people being shot.
If a German walked on the sidewalk, you'd better get off the sidewalk, because they as well could-- could kill you.
There was no law that says that they couldn't.
And it was tougher to operate the store at that time.
And I used to see people sitting on the streets begging and-- for food, and also emaciated and so on.
It didn't touch me that much, but I observed it.
No, no.
But apparently they must have known people in the administration because otherwise they couldn't possibly function in their business.
It was all-- you know, one day you're in business, the next day you were not.
But they functioned.
And then 1942, as I said, things started becoming worse.
They were pulling people out of the ghetto, sending them on transports.
But the farmers had the right to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had the right to elect ghetto people to work on their farms.
Those were the large farmers that were connected to the German government.
And my mom and dad and my sister and myself, and maybe 30 other families, were chosen by this particular farmer to work on his farm.
Now, that farm was a whole village that he owned.
It goes back to the fiefdoms, that type of thing.
And you paid him because you were out of the ghetto.
And that happened just before the Jewish holidays, probably in June of '42.
And we were there for a couple of months.
We lived in one big barn.
And my parents would go in the fields and work.
Hung around.
I don't know.
Just hung around, I think.
I used to go with them or hang around the barn, hang around, do nonsense things.
But then came the Jewish holidays in 1942.
Rosh Hashanah.
That's-- Rosh Hashanah comes out in September, I believe, and Yom Kippur comes out either the end of September or October.
And everybody wanted to go home for the holidays.
My mother expected that bad things were happening.
She made a deal with a Polish woman to take my sister and hide her.
Irene.
Ita--
--was her Jewish name, my older sister.
So they left her on a farm.
Myself, my mom and dad, and my younger sister came back for the holidays, for Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
And the Day of Atonement, the day after, the morning after the Day of Atonement, the ghetto was invaded by the SS and Ukrainians, Ukrainian soldiers, and they started the liquidation.
I remember my mother waking me up and telling me to get dressed in a hurry.
We had to go in a hiding place because they were killing Jews, and they were liquidating the ghetto.
In our building, there was a double wall on the second floor.
It's like a room that was hidden from sight, and you can only go in through the attic.
OK?
So all of us, including our neighbors, went into that hiding place, into that big room.
There probably were about 30 to 40 people, men, women, children, and infants.
And that day and that night, all you hear was shooting and screaming.
And in back of our yard there was a-- in back of our house there was a-- a-- a-- a field.
And they dug a big grave, and they would march the people down there and shoot some of the people.
Some were marched to the train to go to Treblinka.
I don't know what reason or rhyme was used for that.
We were in that hiding place.
And I guess my first encounter with death was in the hiding place.
Children started to cry.
And the adults were telling the mothers to stifle their children.
And if they didn't stifle them, we would all be found out.
And there was a woman sitting next to me with a small child, maybe two people away, that quieted her child by choking it, by smothering the child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And pretty soon we were found out anyway.
They were shooting into the hiding place.
And they opened up the opening to the hiding place, and Juden raus, you know?
And we all were-- What does Juden raus mean?
Juden raus, Jews out.
And we were all herded out into the street against a building, and my mother was separated from us.
My dad and I and my little sister were marched against that wall while the rest of the people were being brought out.
